skill.name: browser-discovery
skill.description: Browser automation guidance for discovering documentation pages when static fetching cannot read JavaScript-rendered sites.
skill.summary: Detects browser tooling and guides documentation discovery on JavaScript-rendered sites.
skill.author: Consiliency

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell, network, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found the files are Markdown instructions and examples rather than executable installer code. No malicious intent or prompt-injection text was found, but the skill legitimately directs agents to use browser automation, local Chrome debugging, network navigation, and JavaScript evaluation, so it should publish with warnings.

medium_findings.title: Browser Automation With Local Command Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs agents to run local browser tooling, Cursor CLI commands, Chrome debugging commands, and a Playwright wrapper. This is legitimate for documentation discovery, but it can execute local programs and should require user consent before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and semantically part of the intended workflow. They are in Markdown examples, not auto-executed code, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Chrome Remote Debugging Guidance
medium_findings.description: The Playwright cookbook tells users to expose Chrome debugging on port 9222 and connect automation to it. This can allow browser control if exposed beyond localhost, so users need clear scoping and isolation warnings.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file directly documents Chrome debugging and localhost access. The examples appear intended for local use, which lowers severity but does not remove the security concern.

medium_findings.title: JavaScript Evaluation During Page Extraction
medium_findings.description: The Playwright guidance includes evaluating JavaScript in a loaded page to extract links. This can be appropriate for scraping documentation, but it expands the trust boundary to page scripts and browser state.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evaluate capability is documented as a normal feature and not hidden. It is still a meaningful browser automation risk because it executes code in page context.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Example URLs
low_findings.description: Several examples navigate to a documentation site and localhost debugging endpoints. These are transparent examples, not hidden exfiltration endpoints, but users should replace them with approved documentation targets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible examples used for documentation discovery and local CDP checks. I found no evidence that credentials or private data are sent to them.

low_findings.title: Filesystem Scanner Hit Is Benign Redirection
low_findings.description: The filesystem finding points to command output redirection to a standard device file during a Chrome readiness check. This is a common shell pattern and does not read or modify user files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited location is a Markdown shell example that redirects output to a standard null device. There is no evidence of file harvesting, persistence, or destructive file access.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography alerts occur in Markdown prose, tables, or browser command descriptions rather than cryptographic code. No hashing, cipher selection, or credential handling implementation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanned files are Markdown guidance files, and the cited lines do not define cryptographic algorithms. This strongly supports a static-analysis false positive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Browser Debugging Endpoint
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow uses Chrome DevTools Protocol on port 9222 to drive a browser. This should stay bound to local, isolated browser profiles.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly documented as a prerequisite and troubleshooting step. It is expected behavior for this skill but still security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser JavaScript Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: The cookbook includes JavaScript evaluation for extracting navigation links from a page. This should be restricted to trusted documentation sites.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The evaluate command is explicitly described and is not hidden. The risk depends on the target page and browser state.

dangerous_patterns.title: CLI Delegation With Prompt Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The Cursor CLI example includes a homepage variable inside a shell command prompt. Users should validate URL inputs before using them in copied shell commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are documentation, not executable source, and the variable is inside a natural-language prompt. It is still worth warning because copied shell examples can mishandle untrusted input.

content.value_statement: Static fetch tools often miss navigation on JavaScript-rendered documentation sites. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through safe browser-tool detection and page discovery.
actual_capabilities: Decides when browser automation is needed after curl or static fetching fails.
Detects Antigravity, Cursor, and Playwright browser automation options.
Provides workflows for extracting documentation navigation links from rendered pages.
Recommends accessibility snapshots before screenshots for structured link discovery.
Documents fallback steps when no browser automation tool is available.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not include executable automation code.
Browser workflows require tools that may not exist in every AI environment.
Remote debugging and browser automation need user approval and local isolation.
Results depend on target site structure, authentication, and JavaScript behavior.

use_cases.title: Map JavaScript Documentation Sites
use_cases.description: Discover navigation pages when static fetching returns little content or only client-side application shells.

use_cases.title: Choose Available Browser Tools
use_cases.description: Check whether Antigravity, Cursor, or Playwright is available before asking an agent to browse.

use_cases.title: Collect Structured Link Inventories
use_cases.description: Use rendered page snapshots to collect page URLs, titles, and documentation sections.

prompt_templates.title: Check If Browser Discovery Is Needed
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage for one documentation site.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser-discovery to decide whether this documentation site needs browser automation. Try static fetching first, then explain the result and next step.

prompt_templates.title: Detect Browser Automation Tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser-discovery to detect available browser automation options in this environment. Recommend one tool and explain why it fits this documentation target.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Documentation Navigation
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a page inventory from a rendered site.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use browser-discovery to collect documentation navigation links from this JavaScript-rendered site. Return page URLs, titles, and sections.

prompt_templates.title: Audit a Browser Discovery Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this proposed browser-discovery workflow for security, scope, and tool choice. Identify commands that need user approval before execution.

output_examples.input: A documentation homepage returns a small JavaScript shell when fetched with curl.
output_examples.output: The skill recommends browser automation, waits for rendering, captures a structured snapshot, and returns discovered navigation pages.

output_examples.input: The environment has Cursor tools but no Playwright wrapper configured.
output_examples.output: The skill selects the Cursor browser workflow and explains the expected navigation, wait, snapshot, and extraction steps.

output_examples.input: No supported browser automation tool is available.
output_examples.output: The skill reports that browser discovery cannot run and lists setup options for the user to approve.

best_practices: Try static fetching before browser automation unless the target is known to require JavaScript.
Use accessibility snapshots before screenshots when extracting links or page titles.
Limit browsing to approved documentation domains and close browser sessions when finished.
anti_patterns: Do not open arbitrary user-provided URLs without validating scope and approval.
Do not expose Chrome debugging beyond a local, isolated environment.
Do not use screenshots as the first extraction method when structured snapshots are available.

faq.question: Does this skill browse the web by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides instructions for using available browser tools when the user or agent chooses to run them.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but it guides local commands, browser automation, network navigation, and JavaScript evaluation.

faq.question: Does it require Playwright?
faq.answer: No. It can guide Antigravity, Cursor, or Playwright workflows depending on what tools are available.

faq.question: Can it handle authenticated documentation?
faq.answer: Only if the approved browser environment already has safe access. It should not request or store credentials.

faq.question: What output should users expect?
faq.answer: Users should expect discovered page links, titles, sections, and a short note about whether JavaScript rendering was required.

faq.question: Should Chrome remote debugging be enabled?
faq.answer: Only with explicit user approval, local binding, and an isolated browser profile for the discovery task.
